---
title: "Security services"
description: "What Security Hub / GuardDuty / Inspector / Access Analyzer / Config do — and why Lowkey turns them on by default."
---

Lowkey enables five AWS security services by default for `builder` and `account_assistant` profiles. They cost a little money and give you alert-only monitoring of what the agent does.

## What each service does

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="Security Hub" icon="shield">
    Aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Access Analyzer, and AWS Config into a single dashboard. One place to look when reviewing what the agent's been up to.
  </Card>
  <Card title="GuardDuty" icon="radar">
    Threat detection on AWS API activity. Flags anomalies: crypto-mining patterns, credential exfiltration, malware network signatures, unusual API call patterns. Great at catching agents doing obviously wrong things.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Inspector" icon="magnifying-glass">
    Continuous vulnerability scanning on EC2 / Lambda / ECR. Tells you when the agent's running OS or Lambda runtimes have known CVEs.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Access Analyzer" icon="door-open">
    Flags resources that are exposed outside your account (public S3 buckets, over-permissioned IAM roles). Catches the "oops the agent made this public" class of mistake.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Config recorder" icon="book">
    Records every resource configuration change. Gives you an audit trail — what did the agent create, when, with what settings.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>

## Why they're on by default

Builder and account_assistant profiles give the agent meaningful AWS access. Running those profiles **without** monitoring would mean you find out about problems by reading the bill. These services are **alert-only** by default — they never block the agent; they just give you visibility.

Personal assistant profile has **no AWS write access at all**, so there's nothing to monitor. Services are off by default to keep cost near zero.

## Approximate cost

Very rough; varies by region and activity:

| Service | Small single-agent install | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Security Hub | $1–5 / month | Per-finding + per-check |
| GuardDuty | $5–20 / month | VPC flow logs + CloudTrail + DNS logs |
| Inspector | $1–3 / month | Per-resource per-scan |
| Access Analyzer | $0 | Free tier covers typical use |
| Config recorder | $2–10 / month | Per-recording + per-evaluation |
| **Total** | **~$10–40 / month** | Mostly GuardDuty |

Your AWS bill is the source of truth.

## Opting out (advanced mode)

You can disable any subset:

```bash
curl -sfL install.lowkey.run | bash
# → pick Advanced mode
# → answer "no" to the ones you want to skip
```

Or deploy the template directly and override the `Enable*` parameters.

## Opting in for personal_assistant

Also possible in advanced mode. You might want GuardDuty even on a pure-Bedrock agent just to catch compromised instance credentials.

## Where findings appear

All findings route into **Security Hub** if enabled. Without it, each service has its own console:

- GuardDuty: **GuardDuty > Findings**
- Inspector: **Inspector > Findings**
- Access Analyzer: **IAM Access Analyzer > Findings**
- Config: **Config > Rules > Non-compliant resources**

Routing into Security Hub is the lowest-friction setup.

## Tagging

All findings inherit the `loki:managed=true` + `loki:watermark=<env-name>` tags from the underlying resource, so you can filter to "findings about this Lowkey install" in Security Hub:

```
Filter: ResourceTags.key = loki:managed
```
